figures.caption: Figure 1: Overall architecture of the proposed MSCGC-KAN model. CBraMod first extracts generic EEG representations, MCRBlock-GCN then performs multi-scale causal temporal modeling and learnable graph-based spatial modeling, and KANLinear finally maps the enhanced features to an expressive nonlinear discriminative space for emotion classification.
Table 1: Description of Frequently Used Notations
Table 3: Performance comparison of different methods on the FACED dataset (mean±std).
Table 4: Performance comparison of different methods on the SEED-VII dataset (mean±std).
Figure 2: Confusion matrix on FACED. Major confusions are concentrated among emotionally similar classes, reflecting the local similarity structure of affective space.
Figure 3: Confusion matrix on SEED-VII. The diagonal-dominant structure indicates that the model learns stable decision boundaries for most emotion categories.

Figure 4: t-SNE visualization of stage-wise feature spaces on FACED. The three-stage comparison shows that MCRBlock-GCN and KANLinear progressively improve inter-class separability and intra-class compactness.
Figure 5: t-SNE visualization of stage-wise feature spaces on SEED-VII. Compared with the backbone output, the graph-enhanced and KAN-mapped representations show clearer class separation.
Figure 7: Visualization of the learnable adjacency matrix on FACED, including the learned connectivity heat map, the brain-topology graph, and the ranking of hub channels.
Figure 8: Scalp topography visualization on FACED. Different emotions exhibit distinguishable spatial activation distributions, especially over prefrontal and temporal regions.
Figure 9: Grad-CAM temporal heat map on FACED. High-response regions indicate the temporal segments that contribute most strongly to emotion classification.

Figure 10: Basis-response distributions and projection-weight importance of the KAN layer on FACED. The linear term and multiple nonlinear basis functions jointly contribute to emotion-discriminative mapping.
Figure A.12: Supplementary scalp topography visualization on SEED-VII.
Figure A.11: Supplementary visualization of the learnable adjacency matrix on SEED-VII, including the learned connectivity heat map, the brain-topology graph, and the ranking of hub channels.
Figure A.14: Supplementary basis-response distributions and projection-weight importance of the KAN layer on SEED-VII.
Figure A.13: Supplementary Grad-CAM temporal heat map on SEED-VII.
